nodes.text: V — The Hierophant

THE HIEROPHANT. He holds the keys to what is sacred, and to what is not. Tradition. Institution. The interpretation of knowledge too dangerous to leave in the hands of those who have not been authorized to hold it. Reversed: rigidity. Dogma as weapon. The man who mistakes his office for the divine.
§ I. Maya Daigle, Age 7, Outside St. Jude's Acadian Church
The heat shimmered off the asphalt like angry ghosts.

Maya's itchy Sunday dress stuck to her back. The dress was new. The dress was stiff. The dress had a tag that bit her between the shoulder blades every time she breathed in, and she had been breathing in all morning because the church inside had smelled wrong — like old incense and wet wood and something older than either of those, something Daddy said was just the building settling but Maya knew better, because buildings did not settle in a smell.

Daddy was talking to some men near the church steps. His voice was the low rumble he used when he was talking to other grown-ups — not the bright voice he used when he talked to her. Daddy looked tired. Not regular tired. Strings-cut tired. Like a puppet whose puppet-man had walked away from the show.

Maya traced patterns in the condensation on her sweating cup of lemonade-flavored water. The ladies with tight smiles and too much hairspray had patted her on the head and said aren't you a precious little thing in voices that meant something other than what the words said. The smile had reset, the way a clock resets when you push the little button on the back.
Then he walked out of the church, and even the cicadas seemed to hush for a second.
Mr. Quentin Paul.

He wasn't smiling. He wore a suit, even though it felt like the inside of a dog's mouth outside. His eyes swept over the small crowd leaving the service, pausing on folks here and there, like he was counting sins. Or maybe just deciding who wasn't worth saving.

He radiated something. Hot and cold at the same time. Like the metal slide at the playground after it had been in the sun all day, where the top burned your hands but the bottom, where the slide curved into the wood-chips, was still cold from the rain the night before.

Mr. Paul stopped near Daddy. Clapped one of the other men on the shoulder — a hard, deliberate sound, the sound of two pieces of wood being put together in a way that meant something. His voice wasn't loud like the preachers inside, but it felt louder somehow. Like it pushed the air out of the way.

Mr. Paul's gaze flickered toward Maya for just a second. It felt like being caught doing something wrong, even though she wasn't doing anything wrong. He looked right through her. Like she was just scenery. The kind of person who appears in a photograph without being one of the subjects.

Then his gaze flickered back, and stayed for the second second, and that second second was longer than the first, and during it Maya understood, in the way children understand things they have no words for, that Mr. Quentin Paul had decided something about her, had made some small entry in some private ledger she could not see, and that the entry was not, on balance, a good one.
Then he turned. Sharp. Strode toward a long black car idling by the curb. Moving like he owned the damn sidewalk and maybe the sky too.
Grown-ups were weird. Especially the scary ones who looked like they had swallowed God whole and were still hungry.
She did not, at seven, know the word cataloged. She would learn it later. She would, much later, recognize the feeling for what it had been.
For now: she finished her lemonade, dropped the cup in the trash, took her father's hand when he came down the steps, and did not mention any of it.
§ II. Gloria Reyes, Waitress, D'Ambrosio's Brunch Shift
Gloria slapped down another basket of lukewarm bread, ignoring the imperious wave from Table 12.

Sunday brunch at D'Ambrosio's was its own special circle of hell. The mimosas were too sweet. The eggs Florentine had a hollandaise that broke if you looked at it wrong. The clientele dressed for church even when they had not gone, on the theory that dressing for church covered your bases. Gloria had been working Sunday brunch since 1996.
She also knew when he held court.

Quentin Paul, ensconced at his usual corner booth overlooking the muddy divide between Texas and Louisiana, pontificating to a captive audience of city council hopefuls and nervous-looking business types. His voice cut through the clatter of silverware and forced laughter. Each word wielded like a freshly sharpened knife. He wasn't eating. He was delivering pronouncements between sips of black coffee.

She remembered him from before the sinkhole. Back when he was just another ambitious climber with sweat stains under his arms and hands that lingered a second too long when he handed back the check presenter. Seminary school dropout, they said. Yeah. She could see it.
He talked about tradition. About structure. About the moral fabric of Graustark. All while his eyes scanned the room like a hawk assessing prey.
Gloria caught his gaze as she refilled water glasses two booths down from his court. He did not acknowledge her. Looked past. Focus already on the next carefully crafted sentence.
He was at war, alright. Mostly with himself, she figured. But he dragged everyone else onto his battlefield, and he did not, generally, let them leave.

She saw the tension in his jaw. The slight tic near his eye. The way the cuff of his right sleeve was a millimeter shorter than the left, as if at some point that morning his hand had gripped it and twisted it down without his noticing. Righteousness masking something else. Something brittle. Maybe something afraid.
She hurried back toward the kitchen. The scent of old hymnals and hypocrisy clung to her apron.
He'll be there till three. Same as last week.
He gonna eat?
He's never gonna eat, Hector.
Hector crossed himself. It was his only tic. Gloria had been watching him do it for six years and had stopped finding it funny in year two and had, somewhere in year four, started finding it useful.

She picked up the next two plates and went back out to the floor. She did not look at Table 17 on the way past. She had not, in eleven years, ever looked at Table 17 when Quentin Paul was speaking from it.
§ III. Antonio D'Ambrosio, Mid-Afternoon Phone Call
No, Quentin, listen to me for one goddamn second.

Antonio pinched the bridge of his nose. The phone hot against his ear. Even miles away in New Orleans — even with the Mississippi between them and several hundred miles of bad highway and the entire fucking idea of distance as a category — Paul's voice had the power to fray his nerves like a frayed extension cord finding a damp puddle.

He paced the cramped office above his still-unfinished restaurant, Ember & Ash. The air was thick with sawdust and ambition and the particular smell of a building under renovation that had not yet decided whether the renovation would be completed. Below, the sounds of the construction crew on their thirty-minute lunch — Spanish on the radio, a hammer doing the slow bored work of hanging a thing the hanger had no opinion about.

...simply stating that certain alliances require careful navigation, Antonio. Your father, bless his memory, understood the nuances. Alberto, for all his spreadsheets, seems to grasp the fundamentals...

Antonio gritted his teeth. Bless his memory. Paul was speaking about Dante D'Ambrosio in the past tense as a way of indicating he had begun, in some private accounting, to consider Dante a closed account.
Which meant — Antonio realized with the slow lurch of a man who has just understood something he had been deliberately not understanding — that Paul was beginning to consider Antonio the open one.

He knew Paul was playing him against Alberto. Antonio was the wildcard. The artistic temperament. The potential weak link Paul needed either to control or to neutralize. Antonio had not, until this exact phone call, been entirely sure which one.
Look, Quentin. The situation here is complex. Jimmy Daigle's got things under control, mostly. We don't need any external guidance right now.
A pause on the other end. Weighted. Deliberate.
Guidance. Is that what you call it. I merely offer perspective, Antonio. A grounding in tradition. Something your recent associate, Mr. Daigle, might lack.
The implication hung heavy. Laced with Paul's particular brand of poisonous piety. A man at war with himself, projecting his conflict onto everyone else.
I gotta go, Quentin. Things to handle.
Of course. Just remember who your friends are, Antonio. In these uncertain times.
Click.
Antonio stared at the dead phone for a long moment.

Hierophant. Yeah, right. More like the damn Pope of his own petty kingdom, demanding fealty, handing down judgments from his pulpit. He ran a hand through his hair. The weight of his father's legacy, of Paul's expectations, pressing down. He needed to create something real, something honest, before the ghosts of Graustark swallowed him whole.
Antonio set the phone down. Picked it back up. Dialed Jimmy. It rang four times. It went to voicemail.
Hey, J. Call me back when you got a minute. Got some — got some weather coming in from the east. Nothing urgent. Just — call me.
He hung up. He sat. He stared at the construction plans on the desk in front of him, plans he had loved when they were drafted and had loved less every week since.
He thought: Jimmy is going to take this call, and Jimmy is going to handle it, and I am going to find out, sometime in the next eighteen months, what Jimmy's idea of handling it cost me.
He stood up, went to the file cabinet, opened the second drawer from the top, took out the bottle of bourbon he kept there for emergencies, and decided, on the spot, that this was an emergency.
Outside, the New Orleans afternoon slid sideways into evening. The hammer downstairs stopped. The radio went off. The crew went home.
Antonio drank. He thought about his father. He did not call.
§ IV. John Frank, Late Afternoon, Park near the Old Armory

John sat on a chipped park bench. The Sunday heat was finally breaking, easing toward a humid Graustark dusk that would make the cicadas come out in force. The kind of air a city exhales when it has been holding its breath all day and does not, even in the relief of dusk, fully remember how to let go.
He was supposed to be outlining his next article. The one dissecting the town's fractured psyche post-sinkhole. The notebook on his lap was open to a clean page.

Quentin Paul. Even from a distance, commanding attention. The suit still immaculate, despite the day's accumulated sweat and sin. He walked with that unwavering, purposeful gait — like a man marching toward either salvation or conquest, probably both.
John scribbled in his notebook: Hierophant. Tradition. Institution. Interpretation of Sacred Knowledge. But the rigidity — borders on tyranny?

Paul stopped near the abandoned bandstand. Surveyed the empty park like a king surveying barren lands. John felt like an anthropologist observing a dominant, potentially dangerous primate from behind a screen the primate was not aware of and would, if he became aware of it, immediately tear down.

Paul adjusted his tie. Pulled out a phone. His voice carried faintly on the breeze — sharp, clipped, commanding. Giving orders. Imposing structure on something at the other end of the line that resented, at the same volume, having structure imposed upon it.

Here was a man wielding belief like a cudgel. Utterly convinced of his own righteousness. Yet visibly vibrating with an internal conflict carved into his very posture. The war between spirit and ambition played out in the tense line of his shoulders, the tight clench of his jaw.
Paul snapped the phone shut. Strode away. Left only the oppressive weight of his presence lingering in the cooling air.

John underlined the word in his notebook: Tyranny. He underlined it twice more. Then, with the small unconscious gesture of a journalist who has just decided that a story is going to consume the next eighteen months of his life, he wrote beneath it:

Don't approach him. He's the kind of subject you write around. Witness from the periphery. Build the case in negative space. He'll never give you the interview, and even if he did, the interview would be the worst version of the story.
He closed the notebook.
A pigeon landed on the bench beside him, looked at him in the affronted way pigeons look at humans who do not have bread, and walked away.

In John's coat pocket, against his ribs, the folded napkin from booth six pressed faintly through the lining. He had forgotten he was carrying it. He noticed it now, distantly, the way you notice a wedding ring on a finger you have not yet started to think of as yours.

He stood. He walked toward home, past the old church Maya Daigle had stood outside ten years and three weeks ago in a Sunday dress that itched. He did not know about Maya. He would not know about Maya for some time.
The crow on the bandstand watched him go. The crow had been watching all afternoon. The crow would remember.

The Hierophant, by then, was already three blocks away, in the back of the long black car, on his second phone call of the hour, conducting his small empire in the manner of a man who understood that the empire was not the point.
The fear of him was the point.
The empire was just the means.
